narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and planning to attend law school full-time like most students, you've put together a thoughtful application. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 57.4%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction was driven primarily by your early academic performance in your first year of school, where you ranked in the 1st decile—much lower than the average 6th decile—which had the strongest pull toward forecasting bar exam struggles. Your third-year ranking in the 2nd decile, again below the typical 6th decile, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential factor, suggesting inconsistent upward momentum in your grades. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively as the second-most important factor, pushing the prediction toward success and somewhat offsetting those earlier rankings. Other elements, like your lower-middle family income bracket and LSAT score of 32—below the average 37—played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows stronger college grades but weaker high school deciles, which the model weighed heavily as early indicators of law school readiness. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law amid real systemic challenges; it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your high school record through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Many students in similar positions improve their profiles and succeed on reapplication—your above-average GPA gives you a strong foundation to build on. Keep pushing; law schools value persistence, especially from determined applicants like you.
